title: Quadratic formula
body:
In elementary algebra, the quadratic formula  is a closed-form expression describing the solutions of a quadratic equation. Other ways of solving quadratic equations, such as completing the square, yield the same solutions.
Given a general quadratic equation of the form ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        
          a
          
            x
            
              2
            
          
          +
          b
          x
          +
          c
          =
          0
        
      
    
    {\displaystyle \textstyle ax^{2}+bx+c=0}
  
‚Ā†, with ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        x
      
    
    {\displaystyle x}
  
‚Ā† representing an unknown, and coefficients ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        a
      
    
    {\displaystyle a}
  
‚Ā†, ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        b
      
    
    {\displaystyle b}
  
‚Ā†, and ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        c
      
    
    {\displaystyle c}
  
‚Ā† representing known real or complex numbers with ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        a
        ‚Č†
        0
      
    
    {\displaystyle a\neq 0}
  
‚Ā†, the values of ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        x
      
    
    {\displaystyle x}
  
‚Ā† satisfying the equation, called the roots or zeros, can be found using the quadratic formula,

  
    
      
        x
        =

        ,
      
    
    {\displaystyle x={\frac {-b\pm {\sqrt {b^{2}-4ac}}}{2a}},}
  

where the plus‚Äďminus symbol "‚Ā†
  
    
      
        ¬Ī
      
    
    {\displaystyle \pm }
  
‚Ā†" indicates that the equation has two roots. Written separately, these are:

    {\displaystyle x_{1}={\frac {-b+{\sqrt {b^{2}-4ac}}}{2a}},\qquad x_{2}={\frac {-b-{\sqrt {b^{2}-4ac}}}{2a}}.}
  

The quantity ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        
          őĒ
          =

    {\displaystyle \textstyle \Delta =b^{2}-4ac}
  
‚Ā† is known as the discriminant of the quadratic equation. If the coefficients ‚Ā†

‚Ā†, ‚Ā†

‚Ā†, and ‚Ā†

‚Ā† are real numbers then when ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        őĒ
        >
        0
      
    
    {\displaystyle \Delta >0}
  
‚Ā†, the equation has two distinct real roots; when ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        őĒ
        =

‚Ā†, the equation has one repeated real root; and when ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        őĒ
        <
        0

‚Ā†, the equation has no real roots but has two distinct complex roots, which are complex conjugates of each other.
Geometrically, the roots represent the ‚Ā†

          +
          c

‚Ā†, a parabola, crosses the ‚Ā†

‚Ā†-axis: the graph's ‚Ā†
  
    
      
        x

‚Ā†-intercepts. The quadratic formula can also be used to identify the parabola's axis of symmetry.
